abstract:

Evo 2 is an artificial intelligence-based biological foundation model trained on 9 trillion DNA base pairs spanning all domains of life that predicts functional properties from genomic sequences and provides a rich generative model for researchers in biology.

title: DICER cleavage fidelity is governed by 5′-end binding pockets
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10211-5

Cryo-electron microscopy and massively parallel assays shed light on the mechanism by which DICER, a key enzyme in the RNase III family, cleaves RNA at precise locations to produce small RNAs.

title: Limited thermal tolerance in tropical insects and its genomic signature
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10155-w

A survey of tropical insect populations and thermal tolerance limits indicates that species from lowland areas have low capacity to survive increased temperatures, and that thermal tolerance is limited by fundamental properties of protein architecture.

title: A metabolic alarmin from keratinocytes potentiates systemic humoral immunity
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10167-6

Farnesyl pyrophosphate—a mevalonate pathway metabolic intermediate—is an endogenous alarmin that enhances IgG antibody responses through keratinocyte-derived IL-6 and CCL20.

title: Long-term thrombus-free left atrial appendage occlusion via magnetofluids
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-025-10091-1

In the presence of a sufficient magnetic field, magnetofluids can resist high-speed blood flow, offering a personalized and complete strategy for left atrial appendage occlusion.

title: Lipid metabolism drives dietary effects on T cell ferroptosis and immunity
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10193-4

The susceptibility of mouse and human T cells to ferroptosis is determined by the balance of systemic polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fatty acids, highlighting a key role for lipid metabolism and dietary composition in regulating T cell function.

title: Merlin: a computed tomography vision–language foundation model and dataset
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10181-8

Merlin, a vision–language foundation model trained on a large dataset of paired CT scans, patient record data and radiology reports, demonstrates strong performance across model architectures, diagnostic and prognostic tasks, and external sites.

title: Microbiota-mediated induction of beige adipocytes in response to dietary cues
abstract:

title: The oldest articulated bony fish from the early Silurian period
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10125-2

A tiny, articulated, near-complete osteichthyan from the early Silurian Chongqing Lagerstätte, represents the oldest osteichthyan occurrence including microfossils, and the earliest articulated remains of any bony fish in the fossil record.

title: Bulk hexagonal diamond

title: Sea level much higher than assumed in most coastal hazard assessments
abstract:

abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10182-7

abstract:

abstract:

Cryogenic electron microscopy reveals how dCas12f with σE recruits RNAP to targeted DNA, initiating transcription at a fixed downstream distance, bypassing canonical −35 recognition and stabilizing the −10 element in an unusual manner.

abstract:

title: Largest Silurian fish illuminates the origin of osteichthyan characters
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-025-10008-y

title: Precancerous niche remodelling dictates nascent tumour persistence
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10157-8

abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00377-3

title: Stress-hormone signalling protects spreading cancer cells from immune system
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00650-5

Metastasis occurs when cancer cells spread to other organs and escape elimination by the immune system. Analysis reveals that these cells resist killing by immune cells by harnessing stress-hormone signalling through the glucocorticoid receptor, suggesting a potential therapeutic target for preventing metastasis.

abstract:

title: A genetic switch turns off parental behaviour and drives infanticide in male striped mice
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00656-z

Male parenting in mammals is highly variable, ranging from caring to neglectful and abusive. The brain and behaviour of the African striped mouse reveal that, in males, the social environment and expression level of the Agouti gene affect whether the animals will be hostile or caring towards pups.

abstract:

abstract:

abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00661-2

title: Skin cells boost distant antibody responses
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00379-1

How the skin enables immune defences is not fully clear. Now a pathway has been found in skin cells that boosts the production of antibodies to protect the whole body.

title: How a mathematician is cracking open Mexico’s powerful drug cartels
abstract:

abstract:

title: Radiology AI makes consistent diagnoses using 3D images from different health centres
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00645-2

To meet the growing demand for radiology artificial-intelligence tools, a 3D vision–language model called Merlin was trained on abdominal computed-tomography scans, radiology reports and electronic health records. Merlin demonstrated stronger off-the-shelf performance than did other vision–language models across three hospital sites distinct from the initial training centre, highlighting its potential for broader clinical adoption.

title: Daily briefing: The return of the snail — the month’s best science images
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00751-1

The most jaw-dropping science images from February. Plus, whether cancer blood tests actually work and what we lose when we can’t see the stars.

title: Hunt for reactive metabolites uncovers unusual chemistry in a human pathogen
abstract:
Nature, Published online: 04 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00652-3

A workflow was developed to selectively capture bacterially produced compounds containing a reactive diazo chemical group. This enabled the discovery of two diazo-containing molecules from a bacterium that causes lung disease. Investigation of the bacterial synthesis of these molecules revealed an enzyme that constructs the diazo group, with broad synthetic applications.

title: New Strides Made on Deceptively Simple ‘Lonely Runner’ Problem
abstract:
A straightforward conjecture about runners moving around a track turns out to be equivalent to many complex mathematical questions. Three new proofs mark the first significant progress on the problem in decades.

The post New Strides Made on Deceptively Simple ‘Lonely Runner’ Problem first appeared on Quanta Magazine

abstract:

title: What Crystals Older Than the Sun Reveal About the Start of the Solar System
abstract:

title: NASA’s DART spacecraft changed an asteroid’s orbit around the sun
abstract: A 2022 NASA mission changed the orbit of the asteroid Dimorphos around its companion. New data shows their joint orbit around the sun also changed.

title: The remarkable brains of ‘SuperAgers’ hold clues about how we age
abstract: A new study reports signs that nerve cells in the brain keep dividing over the decades. It’s not so simple.

title: Robots with fingernails can grasp thin edges
abstract: A robotic hand with fingernail-like tips lets robots peel fruit, open lids and pick up thin, flat objects with more precise, human-like dexterity.

title: A koala population’s rapid rebound may let it escape inbreeding’s perils
abstract: As koalas in southern Australia have grown from a few hundred to almost half a million, the marsupials show signs of regaining lost genetic variation.

title: This molecule puts a new twist on the Möbius strip
abstract: A molecule made of carbon and chlorine is half as twisty as the paper loops common in math classes.

title: Chickpeas can grow in moon dirt and make seeds
abstract: Chickpeas produced seeds in simulated lunar soil, offering clues for future space farming.

title: A Titan collision may link Saturn’s tilt, its moon Hyperion and its rings
abstract: A new study proposes that a crash between Titan and another moon spawned Hyperion and, much later, destabilized Saturn’s inner moons into rings.

title: Hundreds of studies have missed how much the oceans are rising
abstract: A widely used method to calculate sea level rise may have missed up to a century of change, so the risks could hit home for millions sooner than thought.

title: A chemical ‘Goldilocks zone’ may limit which planets can host life
abstract: Life needs nutrients like nitrogen and phosphorus. But without the right balance of oxygen, these elements get locked away in planets’ cores.

title: Cockroaches that eat each other’s wings turn into a fierce fighting force
abstract: The wood-feeding cockroach’s cannibalistic love bites lead to a lasting bond. Afterward, the pair prefer each other over all other roaches.

title: The right sounds may turn sleep into a problem-solving tool
abstract: Lucid dreamers who heard puzzle-linked soundtracks while sleeping were more likely to solve those unsolved problems the next day.

title: Over 40? Your rotator cuff probably looks a little rough
abstract: MRI scans of over 600 Finnish adults found that nearly all had frayed, torn or otherwise abnormal rotator cuffs — yet most had no symptoms.

title: Simulations of your gut may predict which probiotics will stick
abstract: A “digital gut” predicted which probiotics and high‑fiber diets would take hold in people's guts and produce healthier outcomes.

title: A rising percentage of U.S. teens aren’t getting enough sleep
abstract: Teens need eight to 10 hours of sleep each night. A large majority get less than that, according to a national survey of U.S. high school students.

title: The ancient human ancestor ‘Little Foot’ gets a new face
abstract: A new digital reconstruction of the face of an early Australopithecus specimen helps add details about the origins of our own species.
